citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
One brown and black male dairy goat born June 2025 was found to be underweight by the inspector. The hip bones and
ribs were easily felt below the animals hair coat. Additionally, this animal has a small soft tissue wound the size of a dime
in the stages of healing as well as a small amount of fecal material in the hair of it's tail. When the inspector asked the
facility representative if they had noticed this animal's health changes, they stated they had not. The goat has free access
to hay and fed grain daily. The inspector observed the whole herd eat grain and no feeding competition was observed. An
underweight body condition could indicate an underlying health issue and if left untreated could result in the distress or
death of the animal.
The facility must ensure they conduct daily observation of all animals to assess their health and well-being and that a
mechanism of direct and frequent communication is required so that timely and accurate information on problems of
animal health, behavior, and well-being is conveyed to the attending veterinarian. This animal must be evaluated by a
licensed veterinarian for an accurate diagnosis and to develop an effective treatment plan.

Several sharp points were present in the outdoor primary enclosure for 12 goats. New wire fencing had been installed
with metal baling wire to attach the fence to metal posts. In several places, the baling wire is protruding several inches
into the enclosure at the height of the goats with a sharp pointed end. Sharp points may cause injury to the animals. The
housing facilities shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and
to contain the animals.
This is a 1st Re-License Inspection for a Class “C” license. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the
applicant.
All items must be in compliance within 2 more inspection(s) or by 6-April-2026 or the applicant will forfeit the license fee
and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection.
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until it expires.
n

narrative:
2.40(b)(3) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
One brown and black male dairy goat born June 2025 was found to be underweight by the inspector. The hip bones and 
ribs were easily felt below the animals hair coat. Additionally, this animal has a small soft tissue wound the size of a dime 
in the stages of healing as well as a small amount of fecal material in the hair of it's tail. When the inspector asked the 
facility representative if they had noticed this animal's health changes, they stated they had not. The goat has free access 
to hay and fed grain daily. The inspector observed the whole herd eat grain and no feeding competition was observed. An 
underweight body condition could indicate an underlying health issue and if left untreated could result in the distress or 
death of the animal. 
The facility must ensure they conduct daily observation of all animals to assess their health and well-being and that a 
mechanism of direct and frequent communication is required so that timely and accurate information on problems of 
animal health, behavior, and well-being is conveyed to the attending veterinarian. This animal must be evaluated by a 
licensed veterinarian for an accurate diagnosis and to develop an effective treatment plan. 
3.125(a) 
   
Facilities, general. 
Several sharp points were present in the outdoor primary enclosure for 12 goats. New wire fencing had been installed 
with metal baling wire to attach the fence to metal posts. In several places, the baling wire is protruding several inches 
into the enclosure at the height of the goats with a sharp pointed end. Sharp points may cause injury to the animals. The 
housing facilities shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and 
to contain the animals. 
 
This is a 1st Re-License Inspection for a Class “C” license. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the 
applicant. 
 
All items must be in compliance within 2 more inspection(s) or by 6-April-2026 or the applicant will forfeit the license fee 
and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection. 
 
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until it expires.
